critic_raw:
This TCN vs. linear regression ED wait time prediction design is one of the better-constructed designs but has a data scope problem, a preprocessing leakage issue, and a comparison design flaw.

The dataset is restricted to 'encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months.' This scope restriction creates a survivorship bias: the model is trained and evaluated only on patients who are established in the healthcare system, not first-time ED visitors. First-time visitors are likely a distinct population with different wait time drivers (unfamiliarity with the system, different acuity profiles, different triage distributions). The hypothesis claims TCN improves ED wait time prediction in general, but the experiment tests only on returning patients. If the hospital deploys this model for all ED encounters, it will systematically underperform for first-time visitors in a way this experiment cannot detect.

Rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is 'computed from the full dataset statistics and applied as a scalar feature.' This means the rolling arrival rate for test-set encounters is computed using a window that may include future test-set arrivals (if the rolling window extends forward in time relative to the test encounter). For a temporal split where the test set is months 21-24, the rolling 6-hour arrival rate at a month 22 encounter should only use month 22's preceding 6 hours — but if the rolling statistic is computed once across the full dataset, future arrivals within the window could contaminate the feature value. This depends on implementation but is an underspecified risk.

All preprocessing transformers including StandardScaler are fit on the combined dataset before splitting. The design explicitly states this. For a temporally ordered ED dataset, the mean and variance of wait times, staffing levels, and bed availability may differ between the training period (months 1-16) and test period (months 21-24) — fitting the scaler on the combined data means the test-period features are scaled relative to a distribution that includes future test-period statistics. This is particularly relevant if there are systematic operational changes (e.g., new staffing protocols, expanded capacity) between months 16 and 21 that shift the feature distributions.

The TCN uses a 12-step input sequence representing the 12-hour history of arrival patterns. The linear regression baseline receives the same 7 features but as a flat input with no sequential structure. This means the TCN has access to 12 × 7 = 84 input values while the linear regression has access to 7 values. The comparison is between a sequence-aware model with 12x more input information and a static model — the performance difference measures both temporal modeling and information richness simultaneously. A fairer comparison would give the linear regression the same 84-dimensional feature vector (the 12 time steps concatenated as static features), isolating the temporal modeling architecture rather than the information advantage.

The paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors to determine statistical significance is valid but assumes independence of encounters within the test set. ED encounters are not independent: high-volume periods produce correlated wait times (correlated errors), and the same time-of-day patterns appear across multiple test days. Using a test that assumes independence will produce anticonservative p-values — the true effective sample size is smaller than the number of encounters.
all_issues_raised: The dataset is restricted to patients with prior visits in the last 12 months, excluding first-time ED visitors — the model is trained and evaluated on a systematically different population than general ED deployment, constituting survivorship bias.

All preprocessing transformers are fit on the combined dataset before splitting — for a temporally ordered split, test-period feature distributions (potentially shifted by operational changes) inform the scaler parameters applied to training data.

The TCN receives 12 × 7 sequential inputs while the linear regression receives 7 static inputs — the performance difference confounds temporal modeling with information richness, and a valid comparison would provide the same 84-dimensional flattened input to both models.

The rolling 6-hour arrival rate feature is computed from 'full dataset statistics,' which may include future arrivals within the rolling window for test-set encounters depending on implementation — the feature engineering procedure is underspecified for the temporal case.

The paired t-test assumes independence of per-encounter prediction errors, but ED encounters during high-volume periods have correlated wait times — the effective sample size is smaller than the encounter count, making significance tests anticonservatively liberal.

The TCN receives 12 × 7 sequential inputs while the linear regression receives 7 static inputs — the performance difference confounds temporal modeling with information richness, and a valid comparison would provide the same 84-dimensional flattened input to both models.

The rolling 6-hour arrival rate feature is computed from 'full dataset statistics,' which may include future arrivals within the rolling window for test-set encounters depending on implementation — the feature engineering procedure is underspecified for the temporal case.

The paired t-test assumes independence of per-encounter prediction errors, but ED encounters during high-volume periods have correlated wait times — the effective sample size is smaller than the encounter count, making significance tests anticonservatively liberal.
